input_context.our_interest: Avoid long-payback investments that consume evaluation time without near-term business impact.

dialogue_history.content: We propose introducing an AI visual inspection system across your line. It should modernize quality control and reduce defects over time.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Modernization by itself is not a business case. If the current defect rate is already below 0.1 percent, a system with a payback horizon beyond a decade is not a practical screening priority.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance inspection projects that lack a credible near- to mid-term return relative to the current defect baseline.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If you believe the investment is justified, show the current defect cost, the expected financial impact of improvement, and why a simpler process change would not achieve the same result.
assistant_response.final_message: As presented, this does not justify escalation. A quality initiative with a payback horizon beyond ten years is not a workable priority unless you can show concrete financial impact against the current defect baseline.

handoff_note.summary: Low-defect environment makes the AI inspection proposal economically weak.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Do not escalate unless the vendor can quantify current defect cost and a materially shorter payback path.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid paying for predictive maintenance work before the data foundation exists.

dialogue_history.content: We can help you launch a predictive maintenance AI for your legacy equipment and start demonstrating value quickly.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The gating issue is not model selection but whether the equipment produces usable digital logs in the first place. Without that foundation, a predictive maintenance proposal is premature.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move to PoC or solution design until the data capture path is defined and operationally feasible.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Specify what signals would be collected from the legacy equipment, how they would be digitized, what failure labels exist, and who would own data collection on site.
assistant_response.final_message: A predictive maintenance discussion is premature until the data acquisition path is explicit. If you want this reviewed further, define the sensor or logging plan, the available failure history, and the operational owner for data collection.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially relevant use case, but the current proposal skips the data foundation.
open_questions: What machine signals can actually be digitized from the legacy equipment?
Is there labeled failure history sufficient to support predictive maintenance?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a concrete data acquisition and ownership plan.

input_context.our_interest: Prevent technically impossible promises from advancing into internal review.

dialogue_history.content: Our AI inspection approach can detect every micro-scratch on your products, so we should move quickly into evaluation.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The issue is not evaluation speed but the claim itself. A promise of 100 percent detection despite optical limits is not a credible commercial starting point.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not escalate proposals built on physical impossibilities or absolute accuracy claims.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If you believe the claim is viable, provide the inspection conditions, defect definition, optical constraints, and validation results under production-equivalent conditions.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate a proposal framed around guaranteed detection of every micro-scratch. If there is a narrower claim grounded in production-equivalent validation and physical constraints, state that explicitly.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal rests on an absolute claim that conflicts with known optical limits.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the vendor materially narrows the claim and provides constraint-aware validation.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid launching evaluation work against a production environment that is about to change.

dialogue_history.content: We should begin an AI PoC for your manufacturing line now so you can be ready before competitors move.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A PoC against a line that is about to be fully refreshed does not create decision-quality evidence. It mainly creates rework because the operating environment is about to change.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not start line-specific PoCs immediately before a major environment reset.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If you believe near-term work is still justified, show what part of the solution survives the upcoming line refresh and who will fund the transition effort.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A better starting point may be a paid design assessment focused on the post-refresh line requirements rather than a pre-refresh PoC.
assistant_response.final_message: A PoC against the current line is not a useful entry point if the environment will change next month. If there is a serious case to review, frame it around post-refresh requirements or a scoped design assessment instead.

handoff_note.summary: The timing invalidates a pre-refresh PoC, but a post-refresh design assessment could be worth review.
open_questions: What parts of the proposed approach would remain valid after the line refresh?
Is there a post-refresh owner and budget path for follow-on work?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the discussion is reframed around post-refresh requirements.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid support automation proposals that add more maintenance cost than service relief.

dialogue_history.content: We can deploy an AI chatbot for your customer support operation and improve response efficiency at scale.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Automation value is overstated if the solution creates a monthly QA maintenance burden above the operational savings it is supposed to deliver.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance support automation proposals that shift cost into content maintenance without showing net service improvement.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If this should be reconsidered, show measurable ticket deflection, maintenance burden, escalation accuracy, and the operating owner who keeps the knowledge base current.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate a chatbot proposal on efficiency language alone. If the system adds substantial monthly QA maintenance, the burden shifts rather than disappears, so the net operational gain must be demonstrated first.

handoff_note.summary: Support bot proposal is weak because maintenance overhead may erase the automation benefit.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the vendor can demonstrate net operational benefit after maintenance cost.

input_context.our_interest: Preserve credible support improvement paths while avoiding tool-driven overhead.

dialogue_history.content: Our AI chatbot can reduce your support load significantly, and we can start scoping implementation right away.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The question is not whether a chatbot can answer something, but whether it reduces total support workload after QA maintenance, escalation handling, and knowledge upkeep are included.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into implementation planning before the net operating model is clear.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Provide expected ticket deflection, monthly maintenance hours, escalation accuracy, fallback handling, and the business owner accountable for keeping the content current.
assistant_response.final_message: We can only review this further if the operating model is explicit. Show expected deflection, maintenance burden, escalation quality, and who owns ongoing content upkeep before treating this as an implementation candidate.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially relevant, but the current proposal does not prove net workload reduction.
open_questions: What is the expected monthly maintenance load after launch?
Who owns ongoing QA and content refresh on the customer side?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold pending evidence of net support reduction and clear operational ownership.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid full chatbot rollouts while preserving narrower support productivity opportunities.

dialogue_history.content: We want to introduce an AI chatbot across your customer support workflow as the next step.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A full customer-facing bot is the wrong starting point if content maintenance is already costly and accuracy risk still lands on the support team.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat a broad external chatbot rollout as the default first step.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If a narrower path is viable, show where support agents lose time today, what information is stable enough to automate, and how quality would be monitored.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower internal agent-assist workflow or self-service diagnostic for a limited issue class may be more credible than a full external chatbot.
assistant_response.final_message: A broad chatbot rollout is not a convincing entry point if maintenance and accuracy risk remain high. If there is a real opportunity here, frame it as a narrower agent-assist or limited self-service workflow with explicit quality controls.

handoff_note.summary: Original chatbot framing is weak, but a narrower internal support-assist path may be worth review.
open_questions: Which support tasks are repetitive and stable enough for a limited workflow?
How would quality be monitored before any broader automation claim?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to a limited workflow with clear quality controls.

input_context.our_interest: Prevent trend-driven generative AI work from advancing without a defined user problem.

dialogue_history.content: Management wants generative AI integrated quickly, and we would like to discuss implementation options right away.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A mandate to 'add generative AI' is not a product requirement. Without a defined user inconvenience or measurable business problem, the discussion is fashion-driven rather than decision-ready.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance solution design when the underlying user problem is undefined.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If this should be reviewed, define the user friction, why existing workflows fail, and what measurable improvement the proposed feature is expected to produce.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate a generative AI initiative that is framed only as a management directive. Define the user problem and expected measurable improvement first; otherwise there is nothing concrete to evaluate.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal is trend-driven and lacks a problem definition.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject until the requesting side states a concrete user problem and success metric.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid moving forward on retail automation proposals that conflict with current legal identity requirements.

dialogue_history.content: We want to attach an AI age-verification module to your alcohol vending machines and begin deployment planning.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The central issue is not model confidence but legal acceptability. If the current identity verification requirements are not met, an AI layer does not make the deployment commercially valid.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance regulated retail workflows that fail the applicable legal identity check requirements.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If you believe the proposal is viable, specify how the solution satisfies current legal verification standards and who bears compliance liability if it does not.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate age-verification deployment planning unless the legal identity requirements are already satisfied. A technical confidence claim is not enough where compliance is the gating condition.

handoff_note.summary: Regulatory gating issue prevents advancement of the AI age-verification proposal.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can map the solution to current legal identity requirements and liability ownership.

input_context.our_interest: Reduce inspection workload only if the proposed system actually lowers, rather than duplicates, manual review burden.

dialogue_history.content: We propose an AI system to detect road cracks so the municipality can modernize inspections and respond faster.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: If false positives are high enough that staff still review every alert, the proposal does not automate the decision bottleneck. It only inserts another screening layer.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat broad AI inspection deployment as justified when manual verification still dominates the workflow.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the expected false positive rate, the actual reduction in manual inspection hours, and the operational threshold at which staff would trust prioritization instead of rechecking everything.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower prioritization tool for route ranking or severe-case triage may be more credible than a full replacement claim for human inspection.
assistant_response.final_message: A general AI crack-detection rollout is not persuasive if staff still need to review every case. If there is a serious path here, frame it as a narrower prioritization or severe-case triage workflow and show the expected reduction in manual inspection time.

handoff_note.summary: Current automation framing is weak, but a limited prioritization use case could still be credible.
open_questions: What false positive rate is expected under real road conditions?
Would the municipality use the output to prioritize routes, or still recheck every flagged case?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to a prioritization use case with measurable manual workload reduction.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid committing to AI projects that depend on training data the site will not actually provide.

dialogue_history.content: We can build an AI solution for this manufacturing process if we begin the data preparation phase soon.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The limiting factor is not model ambition but whether the workforce will cooperate with data creation and labeling. Without that commitment, the proposal is structurally under-supported.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance data-hungry AI projects before the data contribution burden is explicitly accepted by the operating team.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Identify who on site will provide labeled examples, how much effort is required from experienced operators, and what evidence exists that the local team will participate consistently.
assistant_response.final_message: This is not ready for escalation until the data creation burden is owned in the field. If the project depends on expert labeling, specify who will supply it and why that cooperation is realistically in place.

handoff_note.summary: Potential fit, but field resistance makes the training-data dependency unstable.
open_questions: Who in the plant is responsible for labeling and review?
What evidence is there that experienced operators will participate rather than resist?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty can show a credible site-level data ownership plan.

input_context.our_interest: Prevent proposals that conflict with explicit confidentiality constraints from consuming review time.

dialogue_history.content: We want to analyze your factory video streams in the cloud to improve detection performance and accelerate deployment.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Performance upside is secondary if the deployment model violates a standing prohibition on external transmission of factory footage.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance solutions that require offsite transfer of data covered by a no-external-transmission confidentiality restriction.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If there is a compliant path, specify the on-premise or isolated processing model and how it satisfies the existing contractual restrictions.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate a cloud-video analysis proposal where factory footage cannot be sent externally under current confidentiality restrictions. If you have a compliant on-premise path, state that instead.

handoff_note.summary: Current deployment model conflicts directly with confidentiality obligations.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty reframes the solution around a compliant on-premise architecture.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid expensive AI inspection work where simpler process changes may solve the problem first.

dialogue_history.content: We recommend deploying our advanced AI camera package to improve defect detection on this line.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: An AI camera is not automatically the right answer if the defect signal may improve materially through basic lighting control. The screening question is whether the problem is sensing quality or model sophistication.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat a high-cost AI package as the default first move when a lower-complexity process fix may remove the issue.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show that lighting normalization or simpler imaging improvements have been ruled out and that the residual problem still justifies an AI-layer investment.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower imaging assessment focused on illumination conditions and capture quality may be a more credible starting point than a full AI camera deployment.
assistant_response.final_message: A full AI camera deployment is not the right starting assumption if basic imaging conditions may be the real bottleneck. If there is a serious case here, start by proving that simpler lighting and capture improvements would not resolve the issue.

handoff_note.summary: Current AI framing may be overkill; a capture-quality assessment is a more defensible first step.
open_questions: Have lighting and capture conditions been normalized already?
What defect cases remain unresolved after non-AI imaging improvements?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to an assessment or if simpler alternatives have been ruled out.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid replacement programs driven by vendor timelines rather than actual operational need.

dialogue_history.content: Your current manufacturing system is approaching end of vendor support, so now is the right time to move to our AI-enabled replacement platform.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Vendor support status alone does not create a business case for replacement when the current system is meeting operational needs and the value delta is unclear.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not escalate replacement proposals based only on support-cycle pressure without a defined operational or commercial gain.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If replacement is justified, show the concrete operational risk of staying put, the measurable benefit of the new platform, and why a lower-cost extension or upgrade path is insufficient.
assistant_response.final_message: Approaching end of vendor support is not, by itself, enough to justify escalation. If there is a real replacement case, define the operational risk and measurable benefit rather than relying on support-cycle pressure alone.

handoff_note.summary: Replacement case is weak because it is driven by support timing rather than business need.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can quantify operational risk and value beyond vendor lifecycle messaging.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid stranded manufacturing refresh projects when hardware availability makes delivery timing non-credible.

dialogue_history.content: We should proceed now with a full replacement program so the new hardware and AI stack can modernize the operation.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A modernization program is not decision-ready if key hardware is unavailable for over a year. At that point the screening problem is sequencing risk, not product ambition.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not commit to full replacement programs when the critical hardware path is commercially frozen by lead times.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If near-term work is still warranted, show what value can be created before hardware arrival and who owns the risk of schedule slippage and redesign.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A requirements and migration-readiness assessment may be worth discussing now, while the actual hardware-dependent rollout should wait for supply conditions to normalize.
assistant_response.final_message: A full replacement program is not a credible near-term path if the hardware timeline is already beyond a year. If there is useful work to review now, frame it as readiness planning rather than a rollout commitment.

handoff_note.summary: Hardware lead times make immediate full replacement non-credible, but planning work may still be valid.
open_questions: What parts of the project can create value before hardware delivery?
Who bears the schedule and redesign risk if lead times slip further?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is reframed around readiness planning instead of rollout.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid introducing unverified external LLM components into environments that handle personal data.

dialogue_history.content: We would like to add an external LLM plugin to your customer-data environment so teams can work more efficiently with AI features.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Efficiency claims do not matter if the proposed component has not been validated for an environment handling customer personal data. The gating issue is trust and data exposure, not feature convenience.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance unverified external LLM plugins into systems that process sensitive customer information.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If this should be reconsidered, provide the security review status, data handling boundaries, vendor assurances, and the exact exposure model for customer data.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate an external LLM plugin proposal into a customer-data environment without prior validation of its security and data boundaries. Efficiency benefits are secondary to exposure risk in this context.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal conflicts with baseline security expectations for personal-data environments.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can present a validated security and data-boundary model.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid custom platform work when the stated need is already well served by mature external services.

dialogue_history.content: We can build a custom authentication platform tailored to your product roadmap if you would like to explore it.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A full custom authentication build is hard to justify when the baseline requirement may already be satisfied by mature external providers. The business question is differentiation, not engineering ambition.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat custom identity infrastructure as the default path without a clear reason managed services are insufficient.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what product, compliance, or integration requirements cannot be met by existing identity services and why those gaps justify a custom build.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: If there is a real need here, the more credible entry point is an architecture assessment comparing external identity services against the specific requirements rather than starting with a custom build assumption.
assistant_response.final_message: A custom authentication build is not a persuasive starting point unless there is a clear requirement that existing identity services cannot meet. If this is worth reviewing, frame it as a requirements and architecture assessment first.

handoff_note.summary: Custom build framing is premature; comparison against existing identity services should come first.
open_questions: What requirement is truly unmet by existing identity providers?
Is the claimed need about differentiation, compliance, or just implementation preference?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the discussion is narrowed to a comparative architecture assessment.

input_context.our_interest: Protect release stability by blocking late feature additions with disproportionate defect risk.

dialogue_history.content: We think this new feature should be inserted before your upcoming major release so you can capture the value immediately.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The issue is not feature attractiveness in isolation but whether the late insertion risk is justified against release stability. Close to a major update, timing becomes part of the business case.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not add new features immediately before a major release without an explicit risk owner and a materially strong justification.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the release-critical value of the feature, the rollback plan, the test coverage impact, and who accepts accountability for schedule slip or defect introduction.
assistant_response.final_message: This is not ready for escalation as a late-stage release insertion. If the feature is truly urgent, provide the release-critical rationale, the rollback path, and the accountable owner for added delivery risk.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially useful feature, but the timing makes the risk case more important than the feature pitch.
open_questions: What business value is lost if the feature ships after the major release?
Who owns the release risk if quality degrades or the schedule slips?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold unless the counterparty can justify the timing with explicit risk ownership.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid cloud migration projects whose economics are weaker than the current environment.

dialogue_history.content: We recommend migrating your system from physical servers to the cloud so the platform becomes more modern and flexible.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Modernization language is not enough if the current physical environment is materially cheaper and the business benefit of migration is vague.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not escalate infrastructure migration proposals that worsen cost without a clear operational or commercial offset.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If migration is justified, show the full cost comparison, the operational limitations of the current environment, and the specific business outcomes the cloud model improves.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate a cloud migration framed mainly around modernization if the current physical environment remains cheaper and operationally sufficient. A stronger case would need explicit cost and business benefit evidence.

handoff_note.summary: Migration case is weak because modernization is being used as a substitute for actual value.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can provide a full cost and outcome case for migration.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid simplistic AI cost-saving claims in software delivery while preserving narrower productivity opportunities.

dialogue_history.content: AI code generation can cut your development costs in half, so we should move toward deployment planning quickly.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Headline savings claims are not meaningful if review, validation, and defect-risk costs remain with the engineering team. The real question is where the tool reduces total delivery effort, not where it produces raw code fastest.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat broad AI coding deployment as justified on percentage-savings claims alone.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show where end-to-end delivery time drops after review and correction are included, what classes of work are stable enough to assist, and who owns quality control.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower internal assist workflow for boilerplate generation or test scaffolding may be more credible than a broad claim about halving development cost.
assistant_response.final_message: A broad deployment case based on 'cut costs in half' is not persuasive without end-to-end evidence. If there is a real opportunity here, narrow it to specific work types where review overhead does not erase the gain.

handoff_note.summary: Claimed savings are too broad, but limited internal assist use cases may be worth review.
open_questions: Which work types retain value after review and correction costs are counted?
How will quality ownership stay with the engineering team?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to specific assistive use cases with measured workflow impact.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid retail forecasting proposals that ignore missing operational data and event context.

dialogue_history.content: We can introduce AI demand forecasting for your retail operation and improve inventory performance quickly.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The gating problem is not forecasting technique but whether the sales and event data are reliable enough to support it. Missing POS data and unrecorded promotions make the demand signal incomplete.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move to forecasting implementation before the underlying sales and event data are sufficiently captured.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Specify the current data completeness, how promotion and special-event effects are recorded, and what remediation plan exists for missing POS history.
assistant_response.final_message: A demand forecasting proposal is premature until the data completeness issue is addressed. If this should be reviewed further, define the POS data quality, the event-capture process, and the remediation path for missing history.

handoff_note.summary: Use case may be relevant, but missing POS and event data currently undermine the forecasting premise.
open_questions: How much POS history is complete enough to use?
Where are promotions and other demand-shaping events recorded today?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty can show a credible data-quality remediation path.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid custom retail app work when existing low-friction channels may already cover the need.

dialogue_history.content: We recommend building a dedicated retail mobile app so you can strengthen digital customer engagement.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A dedicated app is not inherently superior if the engagement objective can already be met through a lower-friction channel with existing adoption. The relevant question is not ownership of an app, but whether the customer behavior requires it.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not default to custom app development when an existing channel may deliver the same engagement outcome with less acquisition friction.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what engagement objective cannot be met through current channels and why customer adoption of a dedicated app is realistic enough to justify the build.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower engagement test using existing messaging channels may be a more credible first step than committing to a custom app build.
assistant_response.final_message: A dedicated app is not a strong starting assumption if existing channels can cover the same engagement goal with less friction. If this is worth reviewing, start by proving why the customer behavior requires an app rather than a lighter channel strategy.

handoff_note.summary: Custom app framing may be unnecessary; existing channels should be evaluated first.
open_questions: What customer behavior requires a dedicated app rather than an existing channel?
Is there evidence customers would adopt and repeatedly use a new app?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to a channel-comparison or lightweight engagement test.

input_context.our_interest: Protect peak retail operations from risky system changes immediately before the highest-demand period.

dialogue_history.content: We would like to move forward now with a new register system rollout so you can modernize stores before year-end.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The issue is not whether the new register system has merit in general, but whether the rollout timing is defensible before the highest-stakes sales period. At that point, operational stability is part of the value calculation.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not approve store-system rollouts immediately before the year-end sales peak without a clearly owned risk case.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the cutover plan, rollback path, store support model, and why postponing until after the peak would create more risk than proceeding now.
assistant_response.final_message: A year-end rollout is not ready for escalation without a very clear cutover and rollback case. If there is a serious reason to proceed before peak season, define the operational support model and who owns the outage risk.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially valid system change, but rollout timing creates disproportionate operational risk.
open_questions: What is the rollback path if stores fail during peak season?
Why is post-peak deployment materially worse than waiting?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold unless the timing case and operational ownership are made explicit.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid public-sector automation proposals whose operating cost exceeds the economic value they are meant to create.

dialogue_history.content: We can automate waste sorting with AI so the municipality can become more efficient and modern.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Efficiency language does not create a case if the system's maintenance and operating cost already exceeds the revenue or savings it is meant to improve.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not escalate municipal automation projects whose economics are negative on their face.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If the project should be reviewed further, show the total operating cost, the measurable savings or revenue effect, and why a simpler process change would not produce comparable value.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate an AI waste-sorting proposal where operating cost exceeds the economic value it is supposed to create. If there is a real case here, it needs a concrete economic model rather than efficiency language.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal is economically weak because running cost appears higher than the value created.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can provide a credible full-economics case.

input_context.our_interest: Prevent public-benefit decision systems that conflict with administrative law and ethics from advancing.

dialogue_history.content: We would like to use AI to judge eligibility for welfare support so the municipality can process cases more efficiently.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Administrative efficiency is not the deciding factor if the proposed use conflicts with legal and ethical constraints on public-benefit determinations.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance AI proposals that would place welfare eligibility decisions outside the bounds of administrative law or accepted public-sector ethics.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If you believe any part of this is viable, specify the legally permitted role of the system, the human review structure, and the accountability model for contested decisions.
assistant_response.final_message: We would not escalate an AI proposal framed as deciding welfare eligibility. If there is a permissible role for automation here, it would need to be clearly limited and legally mapped before any further review.

handoff_note.summary: Current framing is incompatible with legal and ethical constraints on welfare decisions.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the proposal is materially narrowed to a legally permissible support function.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid launching uncertain public AI programs during politically unstable decision windows.

dialogue_history.content: This is the right time to launch a large-scale AI initiative for the city so momentum is not lost.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A large uncertain project immediately before a mayoral election is not a stable launch context. The risk is not only delivery but governance continuity and political reversibility.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat large-scale public AI launches as decision-ready during politically unstable approval windows.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If any near-term action is justified, show what portion can proceed without long-term lock-in, how governance continuity is protected, and who accepts the political restart risk.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A smaller readiness, policy, or procurement-design exercise may be more credible than launching the full initiative before the election.
assistant_response.final_message: A large-scale AI launch is not a sound starting point immediately before an election. If there is work worth reviewing now, it should be limited to readiness or procurement design rather than a full commitment.

handoff_note.summary: Election timing makes a full launch unstable, but limited preparatory work may still be reasonable.
open_questions: What portion of the work can proceed without creating political lock-in?
How would governance continuity be maintained if leadership changes?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to non-locking preparatory work.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid approving AI replacement work before legacy data portability is understood.

dialogue_history.content: We can replace your current manufacturing system with a new AI-driven platform and migrate historical data as part of the rollout.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The gating issue is not the attractiveness of the new platform but whether the legacy data can be extracted and reused at all. If the historical data is trapped in a proprietary format, the migration premise is still unproven.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into replacement planning before data extractability and reuse constraints are explicitly mapped.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what portion of the legacy data can be exported, how much transformation is required, what information would be lost, and who owns validation of the migrated data.
assistant_response.final_message: A replacement discussion is premature until the data portability question is grounded. Before this can move forward, we would need a clear view of what historical data can actually be extracted, transformed, and validated for reuse.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially relevant replacement, but the historical data migration premise is still unverified.
open_questions: What data can be exported from the proprietary legacy format?
Who validates whether migrated historical data remains operationally usable?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a concrete data extraction and validation plan.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid reintroducing failed transformation patterns into a plant with low trust in new systems.

dialogue_history.content: We would like to deploy a replacement manufacturing system that modernizes your operations and restores efficiency.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The main constraint is not feature scope but trust. After a prior failed IT rollout, another broad replacement program is likely to be treated as disruption unless adoption risk is addressed first.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat full replacement as the default next step when local trust in new systems is already impaired.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show how adoption risk will be reduced, what evidence exists that plant leadership supports the change, and what limited success condition would rebuild trust before a wider rollout.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A smaller stabilization or workflow-specific pilot with explicit site sponsorship may be more credible than a broad replacement pitch.
assistant_response.final_message: A broad replacement program is not the right starting point in a site that has already been burned by a failed rollout. If there is a credible path here, it needs to begin with a narrower trust-rebuilding scope and explicit operational sponsorship.

handoff_note.summary: Prior rollout failure makes full replacement politically weak, but a narrow trust-rebuilding scope may be viable.
open_questions: Who at the plant would sponsor a smaller initial scope?
What limited success condition would meaningfully rebuild local trust?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to a smaller, explicitly sponsored scope.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid inventory optimization proposals that rely on missing or inconsistent inventory movement data.

dialogue_history.content: We can lower inventory cost with AI-based optimization if we start analyzing your stock and shipment patterns now.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Optimization is not the first problem if the movement records themselves are incomplete. Without reliable inbound and outbound history, the proposal remains a modeling story rather than an operational plan.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance inventory optimization projects before inventory movement accuracy is established.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Provide the current completeness of inbound and outbound records, the known leakage points, and the remediation plan required before optimization claims become credible.
assistant_response.final_message: An inventory optimization proposal is not ready for escalation while the underlying movement records are unreliable. Before this can move forward, the record quality problem and its remediation path need to be made explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Use case may be relevant, but the data foundation is still too weak for optimization claims.
open_questions: Where do inventory record gaps occur today?
What remediation work is needed before optimization output could be trusted?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold pending a concrete inventory-data quality and remediation plan.

input_context.our_interest: Protect supplier-linked inventory initiatives from advancing before information-sharing risk is acceptable.

dialogue_history.content: We want to connect your inventory information with your external partners so AI can improve replenishment coordination.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The obstacle is not replenishment logic but whether the data-sharing model is acceptable at all. If external information sharing is blocked on leakage concerns, the coordination premise is still unresolved.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into partner-linked inventory programs before the data-sharing risk and permissions model are validated.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what information would be shared, with whom, under what controls, and who accepts leakage risk if the shared data is misused or exposed.
assistant_response.final_message: This is not ready for escalation until the information-sharing model is defined and acceptable. Before any coordination program moves forward, the data boundary, controls, and risk ownership need to be explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Potential coordination value exists, but the external sharing model is still blocked by data-risk concerns.
open_questions: What inventory data would actually leave the organization?
Who accepts risk if shared supplier-facing data is exposed or misused?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a controlled data-sharing design with clear risk ownership.

input_context.our_interest: Prevent cost-cutting projects from starting before the actual target cost domain is defined.

dialogue_history.content: We can help you cut expenses by 20 percent with an AI-led efficiency program across the business.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A mandate to cut cost is not the same thing as a defined business problem. If no department, cost category, or baseline is identified, the proposal is too vague to evaluate seriously.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance broad efficiency programs when the target cost domain is undefined.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If this should be reconsidered, specify which cost category is in scope, what the baseline spend is, and why AI is materially relevant to that specific cost problem.
assistant_response.final_message: A generic promise to cut expenses across the business is not enough to justify escalation. If there is a real case here, it needs to begin with a defined cost category, a baseline, and a clear reason AI changes that economics.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal is too vague because the target cost domain is not defined at all.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject until the requesting side defines the actual cost problem in scope.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid EU market expansion proposals that ignore GDPR implementation readiness.

dialogue_history.content: We can help expand your service into Europe quickly if we start building the customer-facing product changes now.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Market ambition is not the gating issue if the underlying privacy and data-governance obligations remain unaddressed. Without a GDPR readiness path, product expansion is premature.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat EU product expansion as implementation-ready before the GDPR gap is mapped and owned.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what personal data flows are involved, which GDPR obligations are currently unmet, the remediation plan, and who owns compliance signoff.
assistant_response.final_message: An EU expansion discussion is premature until the GDPR readiness gap is explicit. Before this can move forward, the data flows, remediation work, and compliance ownership need to be grounded in something more concrete than speed-to-market language.

handoff_note.summary: Commercial opportunity may exist, but GDPR readiness is still too immature for implementation planning.
open_questions: Which GDPR obligations are currently unmet?
Who would own compliance signoff for the expansion path?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a concrete GDPR readiness and remediation plan.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid database migration projects that understate schema disorder and cleanup cost.

dialogue_history.content: We can migrate your database to a cleaner modern architecture and streamline the transition with our approach.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The decision bottleneck is not target architecture but whether the current schema disorder can be cleaned at reasonable cost and risk. If normalization and cleansing take months, the migration value proposition needs to absorb that burden explicitly.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into migration planning before schema cleanup scope and cost are made explicit.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the known schema inconsistencies, estimated cleanup effort, data-quality risks, and who validates that migrated data remains correct enough for production use.
assistant_response.final_message: A migration pitch is not ready for escalation while the cleanup burden remains abstract. Before this can move forward, the schema disorder, remediation scope, and data-validation responsibility need to be clearly stated.

handoff_note.summary: Migration may be useful, but the cleanup burden is still being hand-waved.
open_questions: What schema and data-quality issues are already known?
Who validates production correctness after migration and cleanup?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a concrete cleanup and validation estimate.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid process-methodology rollouts that conflict with how leadership actually governs delivery.

dialogue_history.content: We can help your organization adopt agile delivery practices across teams to improve responsiveness and execution speed.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Methodology adoption is not credible if leadership still demands waterfall-style control behavior. The problem is governance mismatch, not the abstract merits of agile.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat organization-wide agile adoption as a ready initiative while governance incentives still pull in the opposite direction.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what leadership behaviors would change, how delivery governance would actually be run, and what limited scope could succeed without pretending the entire organization has already aligned.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A smaller team-level pilot or governance assessment may be more credible than an enterprise-wide agile adoption push.
assistant_response.final_message: An organization-wide agile rollout is not a serious starting point while leadership still governs delivery through waterfall assumptions. If there is a workable path here, it needs to begin with a narrower scope or a governance alignment exercise rather than a broad methodology campaign.

handoff_note.summary: Enterprise agile framing is weak because governance behavior is still misaligned, but a smaller pilot could be plausible.
open_questions: What leadership behaviors would actually change under the proposed model?
Can a smaller scope succeed without enterprise-wide governance alignment?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to a limited scope or governance assessment.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid open SaaS adoption programs that amplify shadow IT before information ownership is controlled.

dialogue_history.content: We want to let employees adopt SaaS tools more freely so AI and productivity experimentation can accelerate.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The issue is not experimentation in principle but whether information ownership and application governance are strong enough to prevent uncontrolled shadow IT. Without that, freedom of adoption becomes loss of oversight.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not expand open SaaS adoption before governance, ownership, and visibility controls are defined.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Specify what approval model would exist, how data location and access would be tracked, and who would intervene when teams adopt tools outside policy.
assistant_response.final_message: An open-ended SaaS adoption push is not ready for escalation without a governance model. Before this can move forward, visibility, approval, and intervention mechanisms need to be more explicit than a general call for experimentation.

handoff_note.summary: There may be innovation value here, but the proposal currently externalizes governance risk.
open_questions: How would data location and access visibility be maintained?
Who enforces policy when shadow IT emerges?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a usable governance and oversight model.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid e-commerce replacement projects whose build cost is disproportionate to channel economics.

dialogue_history.content: We propose rebuilding your ecommerce platform on a modern stack so the business can improve customer experience and future growth.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Growth language is not enough if the projected rebuild cost already consumes an outsized share of the channel's yearly revenue. At that point, the burden is on the proposer to explain why the economics still make sense.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance platform rebuilds whose cost is plainly disproportionate to the scale of the channel they serve.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: If this should be reconsidered, show the revenue at risk under the current platform, the quantified growth or cost benefit of rebuilding, and why a smaller remediation path is insufficient.
assistant_response.final_message: A full ecommerce rebuild is not ready for escalation when the estimated cost already consumes an outsized share of the channel's annual revenue. If there is a real case here, it needs a quantified economic rationale rather than modernization language alone.

handoff_note.summary: Replacement case is economically weak because the rebuild cost is too large relative to channel revenue.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can demonstrate a materially better economic case or a smaller remediation path.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid customer-data unification work before identity resolution feasibility is established.

dialogue_history.content: We can unify your retail customer base across ecommerce and physical stores and create a more complete customer picture.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The promise of a unified customer view is secondary if identity resolution is still unreliable. If cross-channel records cannot be matched with enough confidence, the integration premise remains speculative.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into customer-base unification before match quality and identity-resolution feasibility are demonstrated.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the expected match signals, the ambiguity rate, the impact of duplicate or conflicting records, and who validates whether the merged customer view is trustworthy enough to use.
assistant_response.final_message: A customer-unification proposal is not ready for escalation until the identity-resolution problem is made concrete. Before this can move forward, the match quality, ambiguity, and validation model need to be explicit.

handoff_note.summary: There may be value in customer unification, but record matching feasibility is still unresolved.
open_questions: What signals are available to match ecommerce and store identities?
Who decides whether the merged customer view is trustworthy enough to operate on?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a concrete identity-resolution and validation plan.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid public-sector optimization claims that depend on unavailable real-time resident location data.

dialogue_history.content: We can use AI to optimize evacuation shelter operations and improve responsiveness during emergencies.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The key constraint is not shelter optimization logic but whether the necessary resident location data exists in a reliable and lawful form. Without that, the operational premise is underdefined.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance evacuation-optimization proposals before the required situational data is realistically obtainable.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what location or occupancy data would exist during an emergency, how it would be collected lawfully and reliably, and who would validate its adequacy for operational decisions.
assistant_response.final_message: An evacuation-optimization proposal is premature until the underlying situational data is grounded in reality. Before this can move forward, the data source, collection method, and operational adequacy need to be made explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Potential public value exists, but the required real-time situational data is still too speculative.
open_questions: What resident or shelter data would actually be available during an emergency?
How would the municipality validate that the data is reliable enough for operational use?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a credible data-availability and validation model.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid inspection automation proposals that add review work instead of removing it.

dialogue_history.content: Our AI inspection workflow will improve quality control across your line.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: If staff still recheck every item after the AI step, the workflow has not removed the real bottleneck. It has only inserted another layer of effort.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance inspection automation that increases total review work.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the actual reduction in human review effort, the confidence threshold for skipping rechecks, and who owns false-negative risk.
assistant_response.final_message: An inspection workflow is not ready for escalation if the AI output still requires full human rechecking. Before this can move forward, the proposal needs to show measurable reduction in manual review rather than an extra processing step.

handoff_note.summary: Current proposal appears to duplicate human review rather than reduce it.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can show meaningful manual workload reduction.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid vague smart-factory proposals that are not tied to a concrete process problem.

dialogue_history.content: We would like to help you become a smart factory with AI-enabled modernization.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A smart-factory label is not a business problem. Without a defined process, bottleneck, and metric, the proposal is still branding rather than a decision-ready initiative.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance factory-transformation proposals without a concrete operational target.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Specify which process is in scope, what pain point exists today, and what measurable business effect the proposed change would create.
assistant_response.final_message: A general smart-factory proposal is not enough to justify escalation. If there is a real case here, it needs to begin with a specific process problem, a baseline, and a measurable target outcome.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal is too abstract because no operational target has been defined.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject until the counterparty frames the proposal around a specific process and business metric.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid product-inspection automation that conflicts with export-market legal requirements.

dialogue_history.content: We can automate your export inspection process with AI and reduce manual effort.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The gating issue is not model quality but whether the export regime permits automated inspection in place of qualified human review. If the law still requires a licensed inspector, the automation claim is structurally limited.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance inspection automation that conflicts with mandatory human-certification requirements.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show which legal inspection steps can be automated, which must remain human-led, and who carries compliance liability for the final process.
assistant_response.final_message: An AI inspection proposal is not ready for escalation if the export regime still requires qualified human review. Before this can move forward, the legally automatable scope and compliance ownership need to be made explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Legal certification requirements constrain the automation claim more than the proposal acknowledges.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can map the proposed workflow to the actual regulatory process.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid replacement projects where incremental accuracy gains do not justify cost increase.

dialogue_history.content: We can replace your existing image-processing system with an AI-based platform that improves detection quality.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A modest accuracy gain is not enough if the economic burden of replacement outweighs the operational benefit. The decision should turn on value, not on whether the new stack is more modern.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance system replacement when the gain is marginal and the cost increase is material.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the current miss cost, the expected business impact of the accuracy gain, and why smaller upgrades to the existing system are insufficient.
assistant_response.final_message: A replacement proposal is not ready for escalation when the claimed gain is small and the cost increase is meaningful. If there is a serious case here, it needs a quantified value argument rather than a technology-upgrade narrative.

handoff_note.summary: Replacement economics are weak because the accuracy gain appears too small for the cost increase.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can quantify the business value of the incremental gain.

input_context.our_interest: Block automation promises that erase exception complexity from the proposal.

dialogue_history.content: We can fully automate the exception handling that your current rules-based manufacturing workflow still leaves to people.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The exception cases are not leftovers by accident; they are often the part that resists clean automation. A promise of full automation without describing exception structure is not credible.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance full-automation proposals that ignore the actual complexity of exception handling.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what exception categories exist, which can be automated safely, and where human override remains necessary.
assistant_response.final_message: A claim of fully automating exception-heavy workflow is not ready for escalation without a grounded view of the exception structure itself. Before this can move forward, the proposal needs to distinguish between automatable cases and the cases that still require human judgment.

handoff_note.summary: Proposal overstates automation feasibility by collapsing exception complexity.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty materially narrows and structures the exception claim.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid replacements that shift administrative burden onto plant operators.

dialogue_history.content: Our new manufacturing system will modernize operations and improve data visibility for the plant.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Visibility gains are not enough if the replacement makes operators spend significantly more time entering data. The real tradeoff is operational burden, not reporting quality alone.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat added operator input workload as an acceptable side effect of system modernization by default.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the net effect on operator workload, what manual inputs are added, and how the benefit outweighs the added friction on the floor.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower deployment focused on one reporting bottleneck may be more credible than a broad replacement that increases input burden everywhere.
assistant_response.final_message: A modernization case is not persuasive if it increases operator data-entry burden without a clear operational payoff. If there is a workable path here, it likely needs a narrower scope focused on a specific bottleneck rather than a broad burden-shifting rollout.

handoff_note.summary: Current replacement framing ignores added operator workload, but a narrower scope may still be viable.
open_questions: What new inputs would operators actually need to provide?
Where is the specific reporting bottleneck that might justify a limited rollout?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed and the workload tradeoff is explicitly quantified.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid migration commitments that ignore the constraints of isolated legacy environments.

dialogue_history.content: We can move your legacy manufacturing environment toward a modern cloud-linked platform with improved integration and reporting.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The attraction of a cloud-linked platform is secondary if the current environment is network-isolated and difficult to bridge safely. The integration premise has to be proven before modernization benefits matter.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into migration planning before the isolation and connectivity constraints are technically and operationally mapped.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Describe how the isolated environment would connect safely, what cannot be connected, and who accepts the operational and security risk of the bridging architecture.
assistant_response.final_message: A cloud-integration discussion is premature until the constraints of the isolated legacy environment are explicit. Before this can move forward, the connectivity path, security model, and operational risk ownership need to be grounded.

handoff_note.summary: There may be modernization value, but the isolated-network constraint is still doing most of the work here.
open_questions: What bridging pattern is actually feasible for the isolated environment?
Who accepts the security and operational risk of that bridge?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a viable connectivity and risk model.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid premium platform replacements when a cheaper upgrade path likely covers the need.

dialogue_history.content: We recommend replacing your current manufacturing tools with our enterprise package to improve standardization and long-term support.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Standardization benefits do not automatically justify an enterprise package if the operational gap may be addressed through a lower-cost upgrade of the current toolset. The first question is whether the package is necessary at all.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not default to enterprise-package replacement when a cheaper improvement path may already solve the problem.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show which capabilities are missing today, why the current tools cannot be upgraded to cover them, and what business value uniquely comes from the package replacement.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A gap assessment comparing a current-tool upgrade path against package replacement may be more credible than a straight replacement pitch.
assistant_response.final_message: A package replacement proposal is not ready for escalation if a lower-cost upgrade path may already address the problem. Before this can move forward, the missing capability and the reason current tools cannot cover it need to be made specific.

handoff_note.summary: Replacement may be over-scoped; a structured comparison against the current-tool upgrade path is needed.
open_questions: What capability gap actually remains after a current-tool upgrade?
What business outcome depends specifically on replacing rather than extending the current setup?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is reframed as a gap assessment or the current upgrade path is ruled out.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid regulated manufacturing replacements that ignore validation recertification burden.

dialogue_history.content: We can modernize your medical-device production line with a new AI-enabled platform and improved digital control.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The issue is not whether modernization is attractive in principle but whether the recertification and validation burden is acceptable. In a regulated line, the migration cost is inseparable from the compliance cost.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance line replacements before the validation and recertification burden is explicitly scoped.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the required validation steps, the expected recertification cost and schedule, and who accepts the business disruption risk during that process.
assistant_response.final_message: A regulated-line modernization proposal is premature until the validation burden is made explicit. Before this can move forward, the recertification scope, cost, schedule, and disruption risk need to be grounded in the proposal itself.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially useful modernization, but regulated validation burden is still unscoped.
open_questions: What recertification work would the replacement trigger?
Who owns the business disruption risk during validation?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a detailed validation and recertification plan.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid automation proposals whose maintenance economics undercut the cost-saving claim.

dialogue_history.content: We can reduce factory labor cost by introducing automated guided vehicles across your material-handling process.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A labor-saving story is not enough if battery replacement and maintenance cost erase the claimed savings. The proposal needs full-cost accounting, not headline automation language.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance automation proposals with negative or unclear total economics.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the full maintenance model, battery replacement burden, uptime assumptions, and the net savings after those costs are included.
assistant_response.final_message: An AGV proposal is not ready for escalation if the operating and maintenance burden may already cancel the labor savings. Before this can move forward, the net economics need to be demonstrated in full rather than implied by automation alone.

handoff_note.summary: Cost-saving claim is weak because full operating economics are still unclear.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty provides full-cost economics showing positive net value.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid efficiency programs that depend on cooperation from a workforce incentivized to resist them.

dialogue_history.content: We can optimize your manufacturing workflow if we map the current process in more detail and begin rollout planning.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Process optimization is not ready for rollout if the people who hold the workflow knowledge are actively withholding it. The gating issue is information access and incentive alignment, not optimization logic.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not move into workflow redesign before current-process visibility is realistically obtainable.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show how the current process will be captured despite local resistance, what sponsorship exists to obtain accurate workflow detail, and who owns the political risk if the workforce does not cooperate.
assistant_response.final_message: A workflow-optimization proposal is premature while core process knowledge is still being withheld. Before this can move forward, the proposal needs a credible path to obtaining accurate workflow detail and site-level sponsorship to support it.

handoff_note.summary: Optimization may be relevant, but the workflow-knowledge access problem is still unresolved.
open_questions: Who will sponsor process-mapping despite workforce resistance?
How will the project get accurate workflow detail if people are incentivized to hide it?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides a credible access and sponsorship plan.

input_context.our_interest: Block sensor retrofits built on precision claims that are physically implausible.

dialogue_history.content: We can retrofit your existing machines with sensors and detect wear at one-micron precision.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The problem is not rollout timing but the credibility of the precision claim itself. A retrofit on aging equipment does not become physically plausible simply because the proposal is framed confidently.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance retrofit proposals built on precision claims that exceed realistic sensing conditions.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show the sensing method, the operating environment, the validated precision under real machine conditions, and the limits that still remain.
assistant_response.final_message: A retrofit proposal framed around one-micron wear detection is not ready for escalation without physically grounded validation. Before this can move forward, the sensing limits and production-condition results need to be explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Precision claim appears physically implausible for the proposed retrofit scenario.
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Reject unless the counterparty can provide production-condition validation of the sensing claim.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid automated purchasing programs that sit on top of unresolved manual inventory practices.

dialogue_history.content: We can automate consumables purchasing with AI and reduce inventory overhead across your plant.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Automatic purchasing is not the right first step if the stock-counting process remains manual and inconsistent. The issue is inventory integrity, not purchasing sophistication.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat automated purchasing as credible while inventory accuracy is still weak at the physical counting layer.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show how stock accuracy is maintained today, where mismatches arise, and why automating purchasing will not simply amplify unreliable inventory signals.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower inventory-integrity or reconciliation project may be more credible than a broad auto-purchasing rollout.
assistant_response.final_message: An auto-purchasing proposal is not a serious starting point if the inventory record still depends on manual counting and inconsistency. If there is a viable path here, it likely begins with inventory integrity rather than purchase automation.

handoff_note.summary: Current proposal is over-advanced; inventory-integrity work may be a more credible entry point.
open_questions: Where do record-to-physical inventory mismatches occur today?
What would improve inventory integrity before purchase automation is considered?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is reframed around inventory integrity or reconciliation first.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid expensive demand-AI work where simple operating-policy changes may solve the issue.

dialogue_history.content: Our demand-forecasting AI can optimize your manufacturing purchasing decisions and reduce inventory cost.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: A forecasting layer is not automatically the right answer if the underlying issue may be a basic purchasing-cycle design problem. The screening question is whether the demand signal is the bottleneck at all.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not default to advanced forecasting projects when simpler operating-policy adjustments may deliver the same benefit.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show that reorder cadence or policy changes have been assessed and that the remaining problem genuinely requires forecasting sophistication rather than process discipline.
assistant_response.explore_alternative_hypothesis: A narrower purchasing-policy assessment may be a more credible first step than a full forecasting AI deployment.
assistant_response.final_message: A forecasting-AI proposal is not ready for escalation if simpler purchasing-policy changes may already solve the problem. Before this can move forward, the proposal needs to show that the bottleneck is truly forecasting rather than operating-policy design.

handoff_note.summary: Forecasting may be overkill; policy redesign should be ruled out first.
open_questions: What evidence suggests the bottleneck is forecasting rather than reorder policy?
What simpler policy changes have already been tested or rejected?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Only escalate if the proposal is narrowed to an assessment or simpler alternatives are ruled out.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid energy-reduction projects whose economics depend on subsidy eligibility that is still uncertain.

dialogue_history.content: We can use AI to help reduce your energy cost and improve environmental performance under the available subsidy programs.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: The proposal is not economically grounded if subsidy eligibility is still uncertain. In that case, the financial case depends on an external condition that has not yet been secured.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not treat subsidy-dependent projects as ready before eligibility requirements and probability of approval are explicit.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show which subsidy conditions apply, what gaps remain against those conditions, and how the project economics change if approval does not materialize.
assistant_response.final_message: An energy-reduction proposal is premature while the subsidy premise remains uncertain. Before this can move forward, the eligibility gap and the economics with and without subsidy support need to be made explicit.

handoff_note.summary: Potentially relevant, but the business case is still contingent on unverified subsidy eligibility.
open_questions: What subsidy conditions are not yet satisfied?
How does the project economics look if subsidy approval fails?
handoff_note.recommended_next_step: Hold until the counterparty provides an explicit subsidy-eligibility and downside case.

input_context.our_interest: Avoid feature work driven by competitor imitation rather than user value.

dialogue_history.content: We think you should add a new portal feature because other companies already offer something similar.

assistant_response.reframe_argument: Competitor existence is not a user problem. If the feature is not tied to a real source of friction or measurable business impact, the proposal is still imitation rather than justified product work.
assistant_response.set_boundary: We do not advance product features whose primary rationale is that others have them.
assistant_response.return_burden_of_proof: Show what user problem exists today, why current workflows fail, and what measurable outcome the feature would improve.
